lecture_context.lecture_title: Stanford CS224N: NLP with Deep Learning | Spring 2024 | Lecture 5 - Recurrent Neural Networks

unit.name: Language models and n-gram fundamentals
unit.description: Defines language models formally and begins the classical n-gram solution based on counting local contexts.
unit.summary: A language model is defined as a system that assigns a probability distribution to the next token given the previous context, and equivalently as a model that assigns probability to an entire sequence via a chain-rule decomposition into next-token conditionals [ts=1714s] [ts=1777s] [ts=1821s]. The lecture stresses that language models long predate ChatGPT and underlie autocomplete and query suggestion systems [ts=1862s] [ts=1882s] [ts=1928s]. It then introduces n-gram language models, where a Markov assumption keeps only the most recent n-1 words and predicts the next word from relative frequencies of observed short contexts [ts=1975s] [ts=2106s] [ts=2157s].

key_points.text: A language model predicts a probability distribution over the next word given the preceding context.
A language model also assigns probability to a whole text by chain-rule factorization into conditional next-word probabilities.
Autocomplete and query suggestion are presented as longstanding applications of language models.
An n-gram language model predicts from short word subsequences such as unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams.
The Markov assumption keeps only the most recent n-1 words and discards older context.
Relative-frequency counts of n-grams provide the probability estimate for the next word.
In the running example, a four-gram model would estimate next-word probabilities from counts after 'students open their'.

kp_catalog.name: Autoregressive language modeling
kp_catalog.description: Training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly. Autoregressive models make sequence modeling tractable by factoring p(x) into conditional next-part distributions over an ordered decomposition of x.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because autoregressive language modeling formalizes how training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: N-gram language models
kp_catalog.description: Count-based next-word models that use a short Markov context, smoothing, and backoff.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because n-gram language models clarifies count-based next-word models that use a short Markov context, smoothing, and backoff, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
